did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a MobileNet Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with Depthwise Separable Convolution for skin lesion classification (specifically distinguishing between Melanoma and Benign Keratosis). The model was trained using transfer learning on the HAM10000 dataset, achieving validation accuracies of 0.96 (top-3) and 0.89 (top-2) as reported in the abstract, and 91% (top-3) and 82% (top-2) in the results section.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and implementation of a MobileNet CNN-based skin disease detection system integrated with Raspberry Pi and Telegram. It reports specific performance metrics (validation accuracy of 0.96 for top-3 and 0.89 for top-2 in the abstract; 91% and 82% in results), describes the methodology (transfer learning on HAM10000 dataset), and details the system's deployment and evaluation, which are characteristic of a research study rather than a literature review.

title: MobileNet Neural Network Skin Disease Detector with Raspberry Pi Integrated to Telegram
journal: Shanlax International Journal of Arts, Science and Humanities
brief_summary: This study presents a lightweight skin disease detection system integrating a MobileNet Convolutional Neural Network with Raspberry Pi hardware and Telegram for real-time monitoring. The model, utilizing Depthwise Separable Convolution, was trained via transfer learning on the HAM10000 dataset to classify skin lesions into Melanoma and Benign Keratosis classes. Evaluation results demonstrated a validation accuracy of 91% for top-3 predictions and 82% for top-2 predictions. The system enables low-cost, portable diagnosis by processing images locally on the Raspberry Pi and delivering immediate classification outcomes and notifications to users via Telegram. This approach aims to improve accessibility to early skin disease diagnosis in resource-limited settings by bridging the gap between medical expertise and remote communities.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two primary classes used in the classification task (distinguishing between Melanoma and Benign Keratosis). The paper notes it is a major global health issue and that early diagnosis leads to a 97% survival rate.
One of the two primary classes used in the classification task (distinguishing between Melanoma and Benign Keratosis). The paper mentions it as a benign condition alongside malignant Melanoma.

all_task_types.target: Skin lesion classes (Melanoma and Benign Keratosis)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the system performs 'skin lesion classification' to categorize lesions into 'Melanoma' and 'Benign Keratosis' classes. It reports validation accuracy for top-2 and top-3 predictions (e.g., 0.96 for top-3 and 0.89 for top-2 in the abstract; 91% and 82% in results), which are standard metrics for multi-class or binary classification tasks. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories rather than continuous numeric outcomes.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A dataset consisting of dermatoscopic images for common skin lesions, used as the basis for training and validating the MobileNet model in this study.

use.training: Used for training the MobileNet CNN model via transfer learning.
use.validation: Used for validation to measure accuracy rates and loss values.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, loss, precision, recall, and F1-score metrics without statistical significance testing.

HAM10000.metric: Validation Accuracy (Top-2 and Top-3), Training Loss
HAM10000.notes: The model was trained using transfer learning on the HAM10000 dataset. The abstract reports validation accuracies of 0.96 for top-3 and 0.89 for top-2. The results section reports validation accuracy of 91% for top-3 and 82% for top-2. The model achieved convergence during the tenth epoch. Precision and recall were described as excellent/high, but specific numerical values were not provided. The system demonstrated stable identification of malignant (Melanoma) and benign (Benign Keratosis) lesions.

architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer learning on a pre-existing MobileNet model specialized for skin lesions (re-trained on HAM10000 dataset)

architecture_details.modifications: Additional fully connected layers added after structure adaptation for classification
architecture_details.deployment_format: TensorFlow Lite optimized format for Raspberry Pi

training_details.loss_function: Not explicitly named, but loss values were tracked (e.g., 1.8% at epoch 1, 0.8% at epoch 3)

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: MobileNet CNN with Depthwise Separable Convolution

Preserving RGB color format (no conversion to grayscale) to retain lesion color information
Normalization of pixel values to the range [0, 1]
Application of Gaussian blur or median filtering to minimize noise from low-light photographs and poor camera quality

conclusions: The proposed system successfully integrates a MobileNet Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with Raspberry Pi hardware and Telegram to provide a budget-friendly, portable, and real-time skin disease detection solution.

The MobileNet model, utilizing Depthwise Separable Convolution, achieved a validation accuracy of 91% for top-3 predictions and 82% for top-2 predictions when classifying Melanoma and Benign Keratosis lesions.
The system demonstrated fast real-time prediction capabilities, generating classification results within 2-3 seconds after image submission.

The lightweight architecture of MobileNet allows for efficient operation on constrained edge devices like the Raspberry Pi, significantly reducing computational requirements compared to standard convolutional networks.

The integration of Telegram enables immediate notification of classification outcomes and confidence scores to users and healthcare personnel, improving accessibility to medical assessment in resource-limited settings.
The system effectively bridges the gap between medical expertise and remote communities by providing a scalable solution for early skin disease diagnosis.
Future work will focus on expanding the dataset to include a wider variety of skin diseases to improve generalization and enhancing image preprocessing methods for higher accuracy.
Future enhancements include adding voice command systems and multilingual functionalities to the Telegram bot to further improve user accessibility.
important_remarks: The study is limited to a binary classification task (Melanoma vs. Benign Keratosis) despite the HAM10000 dataset containing seven classes, potentially limiting generalizability to other skin lesions.
The paper does not report the specific train/validation/test split ratios or class distribution within the HAM10000 subset used, raising potential concerns about data leakage or class imbalance.
Performance metrics in the abstract (96% top-3, 89% top-2) differ from those in the results section (91% top-3, 82% top-2), indicating inconsistency in reporting or evaluation stages.

Specific numerical values for precision, recall, and F1-score are described qualitatively as 'excellent' or 'high' but are not provided, preventing independent verification of the model's balance between sensitivity and specificity.
The paper lacks formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals, relying solely on accuracy metrics which may not fully capture clinical utility or statistical significance.
External validation is absent; the model was only evaluated on the HAM10000 dataset, and no independent external dataset was used to assess generalization to different populations or imaging devices.

The system relies on user-submitted images via Telegram, introducing uncontrolled variables such as image quality, lighting conditions, and camera resolution, which are not standardized in the evaluation.

The deployment on Raspberry Pi demonstrates computational feasibility but does not address latency or accuracy trade-offs under varying hardware loads or network conditions typical in resource-limited settings.
The paper acknowledges future work is needed to expand the dataset and improve preprocessing, suggesting current limitations in handling diverse skin types and lesion presentations.
